title: Deadlock & the 4 Necessary Conditions

hook.scenario: Two threads each grab one lock, then each waits forever for the lock the other is holding. The program just... stops. No crash, no error.
hook.question: What exactly has to be true for this freeze to be possible - and which single condition is easiest to break?

mental_model.intuition: A deadlock is four cars at a 4-way stop, each waiting for the car on its right to go first.
mental_model.description: Nobody is broken; everyone is just politely waiting on someone who is also waiting. The system is alive but frozen. Break any single link in the waiting cycle and traffic flows again.

explanation:
A deadlock is a state where a set of processes are each blocked waiting for a resource held by another process in the same set, so none of them can ever proceed.

It can only arise if FOUR conditions hold simultaneously (the Coffman conditions). The key insight: remove any ONE of them and deadlock becomes impossible - which is exactly how deadlock prevention works.

This is different from starvation, where the system as a whole keeps making progress but one unlucky process is repeatedly passed over.

key_points.title: Mutual exclusion
key_points.detail: At least one resource is held in a non-shareable mode - only one process can use it at a time.

key_points.title: Hold and wait
key_points.detail: A process holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by others.

key_points.title: No preemption
key_points.detail: A resource cannot be forcibly taken away; it is released only voluntarily by the process holding it.

key_points.title: Circular wait
key_points.detail: A closed chain of processes exists where each is waiting for a resource that the next one in the chain holds.

common_mistakes.title: Confusing deadlock with starvation
common_mistakes.explanation: Deadlock = a cycle where every process in the set is stuck forever. Starvation = one process keeps getting passed over while the system overall still progresses.

common_mistakes.title: Thinking the four conditions are independent
common_mistakes.explanation: All four must hold AT ONCE. Prevention works by making just ONE of them impossible - usually circular wait, via a global resource-ordering rule.

recall_questions.q: Name the four necessary conditions for deadlock.
recall_questions.answer: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait.

recall_questions.q: Why does breaking circular wait prevent deadlock?
recall_questions.answer: Imposing a global ordering on resource acquisition makes a closed waiting cycle impossible, so the fourth condition can never hold.

recall_questions.q: Deadlock vs starvation - the one-line difference?
recall_questions.answer: Deadlock freezes a whole set of processes forever; starvation indefinitely delays one process while others keep proceeding.

oa_questions.question: How would you prevent deadlock in a system that uses multiple locks?
oa_questions.answer: Impose a global lock-acquisition order so a circular wait can never form; alternatively use try-lock with backoff or lock-free structures.
oa_questions.approach: Anchor the answer to breaking ONE Coffman condition - resource ordering kills circular wait and is the cleanest, most common real fix.

oa_questions.question: A web server hangs intermittently under load with no error. How do you suspect and confirm a deadlock?
oa_questions.answer: Symptoms: threads blocked, CPU idle, no progress. Confirm with a thread dump showing a cycle of threads each waiting on a lock another holds.
oa_questions.approach: Distinguish deadlock (idle CPU, cyclic wait) from a livelock or thrashing (busy CPU, no progress).
